g.data-narration: Welcome to Java's Built-in Exceptions! Let's explore the complete exception hierarchy and learn how to handle errors effectively.
The Java exception hierarchy starts with Throwable as the root class, branching into Error and Exception categories.
Errors represent serious problems that applications should not handle, while Exceptions are conditions that applications can catch and recover from.
Unchecked exceptions extend from RuntimeException. They are not checked at compile time and include common programming errors.
ArithmeticException occurs when an invalid arithmetic operation happens, like dividing by zero with integers.
NullPointerException happens when you try to use a null object reference, like calling a method on a null variable.
ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException occurs when accessing an array with an invalid index, either negative or beyond the array length.
NumberFormatException is thrown when trying to parse a non-numeric string into a number, like converting abc to an integer.
ClassCastException happens when you try to cast an object to an incompatible type, like casting a String to an Integer.
Checked exceptions extend from Exception but NOT RuntimeException. The compiler REQUIRES you to handle or declare them.
g.text: Checked at compile time - MUST handle!
Use try-catch or declare with throws

g.data-narration: IOException covers all input-output failures like file not found, reading or writing errors, or network issues.
SQLException handles database errors, while ClassNotFoundException occurs when trying to load a non-existent class at runtime.
Here is a quick comparison: checked exceptions must be handled at compile time, while unchecked are optional and detected at runtime.
The try-catch-finally block handles exceptions. Try contains risky code, catch handles specific exceptions, and finally runs cleanup code.
The throws keyword declares exceptions that a method may throw, passing the handling responsibility to the caller.
g.text: Declares that method may throw checked exceptions

g.data-narration: To summarize: Use try-catch for handling, throws for delegation, and understand the difference between checked and unchecked exceptions.
